name: Eilean Donan Castle
description: Perhaps Scotland's most photographed castle, Eilean Donan sits at the meeting point of three sea lochs. Destroyed in 1719, it was lovingly rebuilt in the early 20th century.

name: Urquhart Castle
description: Overlooking Loch Ness, Urquhart Castle offers one of the best vantage points for monster-spotting. The castle was blown up in 1692 to prevent Jacobite use.

name: Dunrobin Castle
description: Resembling a French chateau more than a Scottish castle, Dunrobin's 189 rooms make it the largest house in the northern Highlands. Seat of the Dukes of Sutherland, it carries the shadow of the Highland Clearances, when thousands of tenants were forcibly evicted. Its falconry displays are a highlight.

name: Castle of Mey
description: The most northerly castle on the British mainland, Castle of Mey was the beloved Scottish home of the Queen Mother. She restored it from near ruin and visited every summer.

name: Cawdor Castle
description: Despite its Shakespearean associations with Macbeth, Cawdor was built long after the historical Macbeth's death. The tower house was built around a living holly tree, still visible in the vault.

name: Fort George
description: The mightiest artillery fortification in Britain, built after Culloden to prevent further Highland risings. Its massive bastions and barracks remain in military use after 250 years.

name: Ardvreck Castle
description: A picturesque ruin on a promontory in Loch Assynt. The Marquis of Montrose was captured and held here before being taken to Edinburgh for execution in 1650.

name: Strome Castle
description: A small but dramatically sited ruin on the shore of Loch Carron. The MacDonells' stronghold was besieged and blown up by the Mackenzies in 1602.

name: Inverlochy Castle
description: A well-preserved 13th-century castle at the foot of Ben Nevis, Inverlochy saw two major battles. Its round corner towers and curtain walls form a perfect square.

name: Dunvegan Castle
description: The oldest continuously inhabited castle in Scotland, Dunvegan has been the ancestral home of Clan MacLeod for 800 years. It holds the legendary Fairy Flag.

name: Armadale Castle
description: The ruined Gothic mansion of the MacDonald Lords of the Isles on Skye. The grounds house the Clan Donald Centre and Museum of the Isles, set in 40 acres of gardens.

name: Mingary Castle
description: A hexagonal fortress clinging to a sea-washed ridge on the remote Ardnamurchan peninsula — where clans warred, the Spanish Armada anchored, and kings schemed to break the Lords of the Isles. Recently restored, with nine-foot-thick walls and endless ocean views.

name: Old Wick Castle
description: A gaunt Norse tower clinging to a knife-edge promontory above the North Sea â€” one of Scotland's oldest castles and among its most dramatically sited. Known locally as 'The Old Man of Wick'.

name: Castle Girnigoe
description: A spectacular ruined stronghold of Clan Sinclair perched on a jagged sea stack north of Wick. Twin castles â€” 15th-century Girnigoe and 17th-century Sinclair â€” fused into one brooding coastal ruin.

name: Dunbeath Castle
description: A dramatic clifftop fortress on the wild Caithness coast where a brave wife held off Montrose's Royalist army while her husband rode to warn Edinburgh.

name: Castle of Old Wick
description: A gaunt Norse tower clinging to a knife-edge promontory above the North Sea — one of Scotland's oldest castles. Known locally as 'The Old Man of Wick'.

name: Invergarry Castle
description: A six-storey tower house on the Raven's Rock overlooking Loch Oich — seat of the fierce MacDonells of Glengarry. Burned three times across centuries of Jacobite rebellion, its gaunt shell stands in the Great Glen like a monument to lost causes.

name: Castle Moil
description: A romantic ruin crowning the harbour at Kyleakin on Skye, said to have been built by a Norse princess who stretched a chain across the strait to levy tolls on passing ships.

name: Duntulm Castle
description: Dramatic clifftop ruins on Skye's northernmost tip, overlooking the Minch towards the Outer Hebrides. The MacDonald seat on Skye, abandoned after a nursemaid dropped the chief's baby from a window.

name: Castle Tioram
description: One of Scotland's most photogenic castles, rising from a tidal island in Loch Moidart. The Clanranald MacDonalds' ancient seat, accessible on foot only at low tide.

name: Loch-an-Eilein Castle
description: A ruined castle marooned on a tiny island in an ancient Caledonian pine forest — reflected perfectly in dark Highland waters where ospreys once nested on the tower walls.

name: Lochindorb Castle
description: A brooding island fortress in a lonely Highland loch, once the lair of the 'Wolf of Badenoch' — Scotland's most notorious medieval warlord. Accessible only by boat.

name: Rait Castle
description: A rare and eerie 13th-century hall house hidden in woodland near Nairn, scene of a treacherous clan massacre. One of Scotland's oldest surviving domestic buildings.

name: Beauly Priory
description: Founded by French monks who named this place 'beau lieu' — beautiful place — these 13th-century ruins still feel like hallowed ground. Roofless arches frame Highland skies, and John Keats once wrote poetry among its scattered skulls.

name: Fortrose Cathedral
description: The red sandstone remains of a Highland cathedral on the Black Isle — where medieval bishops held power and the Brahan Seer was reportedly burned alive for his too-accurate prophecies.

name: Dornoch Castle
description: A 15th-century bishop's palace turned charming hotel in the heart of Dornoch, just across from the magnificent cathedral. Gothic grandeur meets Highland hospitality.

name: Skelbo Castle
description: A crumbling 14th-century keep overlooking Loch Fleet where commissioners once waited for a child queen who would never arrive — the Maid of Norway, dead at seven.

name: Castle Sinclair Girnigoe
description: A spectacular ruined stronghold of Clan Sinclair perched on a jagged sea stack north of Wick. Twin castles fused into one brooding coastal ruin with centuries of dark history.

name: Bucholie Castle
description: Fragmentary Norse-era ruins on a dramatic sea stack near Freswick in Caithness. Linked to the legendary Sweyn Asleifsson — the 'Ultimate Viking'.

name: Ackergill Tower
description: A striking 15th-century tower house on the Caithness coast, now a luxury hotel. Centuries of Keith-Gunn clan feuds echo through its well-restored walls.

name: Earl's Palace Birsay
description: Substantial Renaissance ruins overlooking the Brough of Birsay in Orkney. Built by the tyrannical Earl Robert Stewart — oppression made beautiful in red Orcadian sandstone.

name: Noltland Castle
description: A bristling fortress on remote Westray in Orkney, built by a man who plotted the murder of Lord Darnley. With more gun loops than almost any Scottish castle and nine-foot walls, Noltland is paranoia made architecture — hauntingly isolated on the island edge.

name: Cubbie Roo's Castle
description: The oldest stone castle in Orkney, built by a Norse chieftain on a tiny windswept island — a raw, elemental ruin where Viking sagas come alive.

name: Scalloway Castle
description: A Shetland tower house built by a tyrannical earl who forced islanders into slave labour — legend says their blood mixed with the mortar, and his execution followed shortly after.

name: Muness Castle
description: The most northerly castle in the British Isles, standing roofless on Unst in Shetland — built by a half-brother to Orkney's tyrannical earl, burned by foreign privateers, and abandoned to the Atlantic winds. Utterly remote, utterly atmospheric.

name: Castle Stuart
description: A brooding tower house on the shores of the Moray Firth, granted by Mary Queen of Scots herself — abandoned for 300 years before rising again as a luxury hotel wrapped in Highland mist and murder.

name: Beaufort Castle
description: The ancestral seat of Clan Fraser of Lovat in the Scottish Highlands — rebuilt in grand Victorian baronial style after being burned in the aftermath of Culloden.

name: Kinlochaline Castle
description: The 'Castle of Butter' — a brooding tower house at the head of Loch Aline, paid for in dairy and burned in civil war, now restored as a private Highland home.

name: Jarlshof
description: One of Europe's most remarkable archaeological sites — 4,000 years of continuous habitation from the Bronze Age to the Viking era, exposed on a Shetland clifftop. Time made visible.

name: Clickimin Broch
description: A remarkable Iron Age broch and settlement on a small island in a loch on the outskirts of Lerwick, Shetland. An ancient fortress in an unexpectedly suburban setting.

name: Mousa Broch
description: The finest preserved Iron Age broch in the world — a 2,000-year-old stone tower standing 43 feet tall on a tiny Shetland island. An engineering marvel of the ancient world.

name: Knock Castle
description: Ruined castle on the shores of Loch Goil, Argyll. A Campbell stronghold with dark legends of spectral appearances and clan treachery.

name: Carbisdale Castle
description: A flamboyant Edwardian castle overlooking the Kyle of Sutherland, built as an act of spite by a duchess snubbed by the establishment. Scotland's most extravagant revenge.

name: Skibo Castle
description: Andrew Carnegie's magnificent Highland retreat, now one of Scotland's most exclusive private members' clubs. Where the steel magnate became a Scottish laird.

name: Castle Leod
description: The seat of Clan Mackenzie in Strathpeffer, said to have inspired Diana Gabaldon's 'Castle Leoch' in the Outlander novels. A living clan castle with ancient trees, its lands were deeply affected during the Highland Clearances era.

name: Ruthven Barracks
description: Stark Georgian barracks rising from a Highland mound — built to crush the Jacobites, then burned by the very rebels it was meant to suppress after Culloden.

name: Castle Roy
description: One of Scotland's oldest surviving castles — a simple 12th-century curtain wall enclosure near Nethy Bridge in Strathspey. Bare and ancient, with a Comyn ghost.

name: Earl Patrick's Palace
description: Scotland's finest Renaissance palace ruin — built by a tyrannical earl so cruel his execution was delayed while he learned the Lord's Prayer.

name: Old Scatness
description: A remarkable multi-period archaeological site near Sumburgh in Shetland, spanning 3,000 years from the Bronze Age to the Vikings. Sister site to nearby Jarlshof.

name: Windhouse
description: Reputedly the most haunted house in Shetland, a ruined Georgian mansion on the island of Yell. Bleak, windswept, and deliciously sinister.

name: Borve Castle
description: A ruined medieval fortress on the north coast of Sutherland, overlooking the wild Borve beach. A lonely sentinel in one of Scotland's most remote landscapes.

name: Varrich Castle
description: A lonely tower ruin high above the Kyle of Tongue in Sutherland, with one of the finest viewpoints in the Scottish Highlands. A short steep climb for an unforgettable panorama.

name: Ardtornish Castle
description: Ruined stronghold of the MacDonald Lords of the Isles on the Sound of Mull. Where the Treaty of Ardtornish was signed — an audacious pact to divide Scotland with England.

name: Tor Castle
description: Minimal ruins of a Cameron stronghold near Fort William, hidden in woodland above the River Lochy. The ancient seat of the chiefs before they moved to Achnacarry.

name: Balfour Castle
description: A Victorian baronial pile on the Orkney island of Shapinsay, surrounded by formal gardens. Built by the laird who transformed the island's agriculture.

name: Wyre Heritage Centre
description: A small heritage centre on the Orkney island of Wyre, near the oldest Norse castle in Scotland — Cubbie Roo's Castle — and an atmospheric medieval chapel.

name: Quoyberstane Stone
description: A Pictish/Norse carved stone in Orkney, one of many carved stones scattered across the islands. A fragment of the deep cultural layers that define Orcadian heritage.

name: Brahan Castle Site
description: The vanished seat of the Mackenzie Earls of Seaforth, famous for the Brahan Seer — Scotland's most celebrated prophet, who was burned here in a barrel of tar.

name: Kilravock Castle
description: The ancestral home of the Rose clan for over 600 years, set in the Highland countryside near Culloden. Bonnie Prince Charlie dined here the night before the battle.

name: Castle Grant
description: The ancestral seat of Clan Grant near Grantown-on-Spey. A massive Z-plan castle with 15th-century origins, one of the largest in the Highlands.

name: Dalcross Castle
description: A restored 17th-century tower house near Inverness, built by the Fraser Lord Lovat. Compact Highland baronial architecture near the Moray Firth.

name: Boath Doocot
description: A remarkably fine 17th-century doocot (dovecote) near Auldearn — one of Scotland's best-preserved, with nesting boxes for over 500 pigeons.

name: Cromarty Courthouse
description: A beautifully restored 18th-century courthouse in the picturesque town of Cromarty, now a museum telling the story of this remarkable Black Isle town.

name: Tarbat Discovery Centre
description: A museum in the restored Tarbat Old Church on the Easter Ross peninsula, displaying remarkable Pictish carved stones found during excavations.

name: Rosemarkie Pictish Stone
description: A remarkable Pictish cross-slab in Groam House Museum, Rosemarkie. Intricate 8th-century carving that reveals the artistic sophistication of the mysterious Picts.

name: Clava Cairns
description: A hauntingly atmospheric Bronze Age cemetery where 4,000-year-old burial cairns stand in a woodland clearing above the River Nairn — the kind of place that inspired Outlander's standing stones.

name: Culloden Battlefield
description: The windswept moor where Jacobite dreams died in under an hour — Scotland's most haunting battlefield, where clan markers still stand among the heather in solemn remembrance.

name: Inverness Castle Viewpoint
description: The red sandstone sentinel of the Highland capital — perched on a cliff above the River Ness where castles have stood since Macbeth's time.

name: Dingwall Castle
description: Minimal remains of a royal castle in the Easter Ross town of Dingwall. Once an important seat of power, now reduced to a tower stump in the town centre.

name: Fairburn Tower
description: A striking ruined tower house in Easter Ross, visible from the A835. The Mackenzie stronghold was the subject of a famous Brahan Seer prophecy.

name: Redcastle
description: Ruins of a medieval castle on the Black Isle shore, overlooking the Beauly Firth. Ancient origins in a peaceful coastal setting.

name: Dun Telve Broch
description: One of the best-preserved brochs on the Scottish mainland, standing over 10 metres high in Glen Beag near Glenelg. An awe-inspiring Iron Age tower in a remote Highland glen.

name: Dun Troddan Broch
description: The twin of Dun Telve, standing nearby in Glen Beag — together they form the finest pair of mainland brochs in Scotland. Iron Age engineering at its most impressive.

name: Bernera Barracks
description: A ruined Hanoverian barracks near Glenelg, built to control the Highlands after the Jacobite risings. Where redcoats watched for rebels amid some of Scotland's finest scenery.
